story.headline: Vaud court annuls immediate dismissal of two Lausanne police officers over racist WhatsApp messages
story.summary: The Vaud cantonal administrative court (CDAP) voided the immediate firing of two Lausanne officers, ruling the racist and sexist messages justified dismissal but the city acted too slowly to notify them.
story.display_body: The Cour de droit administratif et public (CDAP), in two rulings dated 2 July and published Tuesday, found that the officers' years of racist and sexist WhatsApp exchanges were grave and shocking and did justify firing, but that the City of Lausanne waited too long to formally notify the two men that they were being let go, a procedural defect that voids the immediate dismissal. Four officers were originally fired in the scandal, made public at a 25 August 2025 press conference. Police councillor Pierre-Antoine Hildbrand says he will ask his colleagues on the executive to take the case to the Tribunal federal.
story.why: A discipline case that shocked French-speaking Switzerland now turns on process rather than substance; the city could be forced to rehire or compensate officers it judged unfit.

story.headline: Geneva says June's G7 at Evian cost the canton 31.6 million francs
story.summary: Geneva State Councillor Carole-Anne Kast put the canton's G7 bill at 31.6 million francs and said France should cover about 20 million of it.
story.display_body: Geneva State Councillor Carole-Anne Kast said the summit across the French border cost the canton 31.6 million francs: 12.5 million covered by the Confederation, 13 million in police overtime for roughly 1,500 officers, ~1 million for damage, 3 million for other security and ~500,000 for cybersecurity. Geneva still wants France to cover the remaining ~20 million, but Paris has so far declined; Kast warned the standoff could weigh on regional relations. A business federation (FER) estimates 300 million francs in lost economic activity.

story.headline: Doctors bristle at proposed cap on medical tariffs under Tardoc
story.summary: The Swiss doctors' association FMH submitted, at the Federal Council's request, a proposal to cap outpatient fees under the new Tardoc system, and physicians warn it could ration care.
story.display_body: At the Federal Council's request, the FMH (the Swiss doctors' association) has submitted, together with the main tariff partners, a proposal to cap outpatient medical fees under Tardoc, the new per-service billing system that replaces the two-decade-old Tarmed catalogue. Physicians warn the ceiling could translate into rationing of care.
story.why: Outpatient costs are a major driver of the health premiums Swiss households pay, so how the cap is set feeds directly into next year's premium debate.

Physicians warn the ceiling could translate into rationing of care.

story.headline: Economiesuisse reopens the nuclear-vs-solar subsidy fight
story.summary: The business lobby argued that new nuclear plants would need fewer public subsidies than expanding solar, a pointed intervention in Switzerland's energy debate.
story.display_body: The business lobby argued that building new nuclear plants would need fewer public subsidies than expanding solar, a pointed intervention in the debate over how Switzerland replaces its ageing reactors and hits its decarbonisation targets after voters reopened the door to new nuclear.

fields.display_body: The business lobby argued that building new nuclear plants would need fewer public subsidies than expanding solar, a pointed intervention in the debate over how Switzerland replaces its ageing reactors and hits its decarbonisation targets after voters reopened the door to new nuclear.

story.headline: Trump declares Iran truce 'over' as US strikes dozens of targets and Iran hits Gulf bases
story.summary: The US struck 80+ sites in Iran and reinstated oil sanctions after Iranian attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz; Iran hit US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, Trump called the ceasefire over, and Khamenei's funeral began in Najaf.
story.display_body: The US launched strikes on multiple sites across Iran (SRF puts it at more than 80 targets) and reinstated sanctions on Iran's oil exports, saying it was responding to Iranian attacks on three ships in the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint through which roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil passes. Iran retaliated against American military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait. President Trump called the interim ceasefire over and Iran vicious, violent, though he added he would let negotiations continue; oil prices jumped. Separately, funeral events for Iran's slain Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei got under way in Najaf, Iraq.
story.why: After three weeks of a shaky pause, the two sides are trading fire again and the one man who paused it is now publicly unsure it holds, with the Hormuz oil route back in the crosshairs.

story.headline: Ukraine strikes Russian oil and shadow-fleet tankers as fresh missiles hit Kyiv
story.summary: Ukrainian drones struck Russia's Saratov refinery and nine more shadow-fleet tankers in the Azov Sea, while Russian ballistic missiles killed one in Kyiv overnight.
story.display_body: Ukraine's military said its long-range drones struck Russia's Saratov oil refinery and hit nine more vessels of Russia's shadow fleet, the ageing tankers Moscow uses to move oil around sanctions, in the Azov Sea, along with energy sites in occupied Crimea. Overnight, Russian ballistic missiles struck Kyiv, killing one woman and injuring two, with strikes also reported on Odesa.
story.why: Kyiv is increasingly hitting the export infrastructure that funds Russia's war, even as it remains largely defenceless against Russia's fastest ballistic missiles.

story.headline: Marine Le Pen convicted on appeal but stays eligible, launches 2027 run
story.summary: A Paris appeals court upheld Le Pen's EU-funds conviction on 7 July but left her eligible; she launched a 2027 presidential campaign on a 'Trumpian' ticket with Bardella.
story.display_body: A Paris appeals court on Tuesday 7 July upheld a conviction against Le Pen in the EU-funds misuse case yet left her eligible to stand, clearing the bar that could have barred her from the April 2027 presidential election. She promptly opened her campaign on a self-described Trumpian ticket alongside party chief Jordan Bardella, saying I'm not playing for time.
story.why: The ruling many expected to end her career instead frees France's far-right leader to run again, leaving real uncertainty over her actual chances.

fields.display_body: A Paris appeals court on Tuesday 7 July upheld a conviction against Le Pen in the EU-funds misuse case yet left her eligible to stand, clearing the bar that could have barred her from the April 2027 presidential election. story.headline: Venezuela earthquake toll jumps to 3,685 as Caracas moves to reopen its airport
story.summary: Authorities revised the 24 June double-earthquake toll to 3,685 dead and 16,740 injured and announced plans to resume commercial flights from Caracas.
story.display_body: Authorities revised the toll from the 24 June double earthquake sharply upward, to 3,685 dead and 16,740 injured, and the interim president announced the immediate activation of a plan to resume commercial flights from Caracas's main airport as soon as possible. The government avoids counting the missing; the UN warns the true toll could climb far higher, with projections ranging from about 10,000 to 50,000.

fields.display_body: Authorities revised the toll from the 24 June double earthquake sharply upward, to 3,685 dead and 16,740 injured, and the interim president announced the immediate activation of a plan to resume commercial flights from Caracas's main airport as soon as possible. The government avoids counting the missing; the UN warns the true toll could climb far higher, with projections ranging from about 10,000 to 50,000.

story.headline: Chiral superconductivity in graphene gets a full set of switchable flavours
story.summary: Transport on rhombohedral hexalayer graphene reveals a new chiral superconducting state whose chirality and spin-valley character are switchable by several tuning knobs.
story.display_body: Superconductivity in rhombohedral graphene grows out of a magnetic quarter-metal parent state and appears to be chiral: the electron pairs circulate with a built-in handedness that breaks time-reversal symmetry. This transport study on hexalayer graphene reports a new superconducting state and shows its chirality and spin-valley character can be flipped with several independent knobs. Chiral superconductors can in principle host non-Abelian anyons, exotic quasiparticles whose braiding could encode a noise-protected qubit. The result is a control demonstration, not a claim of anyons.
story.why: One of the most-watched candidate platforms for topological quantum computing just became far more controllable in the lab.

fields.display_body: Superconductivity in rhombohedral graphene grows out of a magnetic quarter-metal parent state and appears to be chiral: the electron pairs circulate with a built-in handedness that breaks time-reversal symmetry. This transport study on hexalayer graphene reports a new superconducting state and shows its chirality and spin-valley character can be flipped with several independent knobs. story.headline: A clean proof that quantum proofs are more powerful than classical ones
story.summary: Recent classical-oracle separations show QMA is strictly larger than QCMA; a Quanta explainer this week decodes the result.
story.display_body: The question: does it help for a short certificate to be a fragile quantum state rather than an ordinary bit-string? The classes are QMA (verifiable with a quantum proof) and QCMA (classical proof). Recent results separate them by constructing a classical oracle relative to which QMA is provably strictly larger than QCMA. A February paper reaches the same separation more simply using good error-correcting codes. The honest scope: this is a relativized separation, the standard first step toward, but not yet, an unconditional one.
story.why: It pins down in a rigorous setting that quantum information is not just faster to compute with but genuinely more expressive as evidence.

story.headline: A half-billion-year-old fossil traces where spiders' fangs came from
story.summary: X-ray microtomography of the Cambrian arthropod Urokodia supports chelicerae evolving from megacheiran great appendages and book gills from trunk limbs.
story.display_body: Chelicerates are defined by their chelicerae, the pincer/fang appendages at the front of the head, and by book gills. Using X-ray microtomography to reconstruct the 3D anatomy of Urokodia, a Cambrian arthropod, the authors argue the chelicerae evolved from the grasping great appendages of megacheirans and the book gills from their trunk limbs, filling in the morphological transition that intermediate fossils had left unresolved.
story.why: It strengthens a specific, testable story for the deep origin of one of the most successful animal body plans on Earth.

fields.display_body: Chelicerates are defined by their chelicerae, the pincer/fang appendages at the front of the head, and by book gills. Using X-ray microtomography to reconstruct the 3D anatomy of Urokodia, a Cambrian arthropod, the authors argue the chelicerae evolved from the grasping great appendages of megacheirans and the book gills from their trunk limbs, filling in the morphological transition that intermediate fossils had left unresolved.

story.headline: How a bacterium sneaks inside its host, a clue to how symbioses get started
story.summary: Buchnera, the obligate aphid endosymbiont, secretes an effector protein SyeA resembling pathogen effectors and essential for transmission.
story.display_body: Buchnera is the obligate endosymbiont of aphids, living permanently inside specialised host cells. The authors identify a previously uncharacterised Buchnera protein, SyeA, secreted into the host cytoplasm, resembling the effectors disease-causing bacteria use to manipulate cells, present in the ancestral Buchnera, and essential for the symbiont's transmission. An ancient intracellular partnership appears to rely on the same molecular toolkit as bacterial pathogenesis.
story.why: It blurs the line between infection and partnership, and hints at how the intracellular symbioses that gave rise to organelles like mitochondria could have first taken hold.

story.headline: Interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS carries nitrogen and carbon unlike any Solar System comet
story.summary: VLT/UVES CN spectra give 3I/ATLAS a normal 12C/13C but an anomalously high 14N/15N, the first nitrogen-isotope measurement of an interstellar object, pointing to a cold distant origin.
story.display_body: 3I/ATLAS is only the third confirmed interstellar object, after 1I/'Oumuamua and 2I/Borisov. Using the CN molecule in ESO Very Large Telescope spectra, the team measured a carbon ratio 12C/13C = 151 (roughly Solar-System-like) but a nitrogen ratio 14N/15N about 363, much higher than the ~150 typical of Solar System comets. Because these ratios are set by formation temperature and radiation, the anomalous nitrogen points to a cold, distant birthplace. It is the first nitrogen-isotope measurement for any interstellar object and extends an earlier July study that found 3I/ATLAS's water is extremely deuterium-rich.
story.why: Interstellar objects are our only direct physical samples of other planetary systems, and 3I/ATLAS keeps refusing to look like anything from our own.

fields.display_body: 3I/ATLAS is only the third confirmed interstellar object, after 1I/'Oumuamua and 2I/Borisov. Using the CN molecule in ESO Very Large Telescope spectra, the team measured a carbon ratio 12C/13C = 151 (roughly Solar-System-like) but a nitrogen ratio 14N/15N about 363, much higher than the ~150 typical of Solar System comets. Because these ratios are set by formation temperature and radiation, the anomalous nitrogen points to a cold, distant birthplace. It is the first nitrogen-isotope measurement for any interstellar object and extends an earlier July study that found 3I/ATLAS's water is extremely deuterium-rich.

story.headline: Quaoar's shape and density, nailed down by 14 years of stars blinking out
story.summary: 28 stellar occultations of trans-Neptunian object Quaoar over 2018-2025 fit an oblate ellipsoid (~1094 km diameter) and confirm no global atmosphere.
story.display_body: When a distant body passes in front of a background star, timing that blink from several sites gives its size and shape far more precisely than direct imaging. Combining 28 occultations of the trans-Neptunian object Quaoar from 2018-2025 with nine earlier events, the authors fit an oblate ellipsoid: equatorial radii ~566 km, polar radius ~511 km, equivalent diameter 1094 +/- 5 km, geometric albedo ~0.13. The shape constraint sharpens Quaoar's bulk density and confirms it holds essentially no global atmosphere, notable for a world hosting a ring beyond where theory says rings should survive.
story.why: Precise bulk properties for individual Kuiper-belt worlds are the raw material for testing how the outer Solar System's dwarf planets formed.
